core_behavior: The system must validate passwords to ensure they meet specific security criteria.
A password is considered valid if it is between 8 and 128 characters in length.
A password is considered valid if it contains at least one uppercase letter (A-Z).
A password is considered valid if it contains at least one lowercase letter (a-z).
A password is considered valid if it contains at least one digit (0-9).
A password is considered valid if it contains at least one special character (e.g., !, @, #, $, %, ^, &, *, (, ), etc.).
inputs: The system accepts a single input: a string representing the password to be validated.
outputs: The system returns a boolean value: `true` if the password meets all the specified criteria.
The system returns a boolean value: `false` if the password fails to meet any of the specified criteria.
assumptions: The special characters considered valid are those typically found on a standard US keyboard: `!@#$%^&*()-_=+[]{}|;:'",.<>?/`.
The system is case-sensitive, meaning 'A' and 'a' are considered different characters.
The input password is a plain text string and does not contain any non-printable characters.
The system does not need to provide feedback on which specific criteria were not met if the password is invalid.
failure_modes: If the input is not a string, the system will return `false`.
If the input is `null` or an empty string, the system will return `false`.
If the input exceeds 128 characters, the system will return `false`.
edge_cases: A password exactly 8 characters long that meets all other criteria should return `true`.
A password exactly 128 characters long that meets all other criteria should return `true`.
A password with only one character type (e.g., all lowercase letters) should return `false`.
A password with no special characters should return `false`.
A password with spaces or non-printable characters should be considered invalid and return `false`.
security_constraints: The system must not store or log passwords in any form to ensure user privacy and security.
The system should be resistant to injection attacks and should sanitize inputs to prevent security vulnerabilities.
performance_constraints: The system should validate passwords in real-time, with a response time of less than 100 milliseconds for typical use cases.
The system should handle up to 1000 password validation requests per second without degradation in performance.
